1: "Then YHWH said to me, 'Take a large tablet and write on it in common characters, 'Maher-shalal-hash-baz.'" The writing of prophetic judgment on a large public tablet is an enacted oracle. Revelation 1:11 commissions John to "write what you see in a book" — the same writing-as-public-witness function. The name Maher-shalal-hash-baz is the judgment counterpart to the salvation of "Immanuel" in Isaiah 7:14; both are enacted in child-naming. Jesus's name (Matthew 1:21) is the ultimate prophetic naming — the word of God compressed into a personal identity.
2: "And I will get reliable witnesses, Uriah the priest and Zechariah the son of Jeberechiah, to attest for me." The two witnesses required by Torah (Deuteronomy 17:6; Isaiah 19:15) are the legal foundation for the prophecy's credibility. Jesus cites the two-witness principle in John 8:17-18: "I am the one who bears witness about myself, and the Father who sent me bears witness about me." Revelation's two witnesses (Revelation 11:3-4) carry the same prophetic authentication function. At the passion, the false witnesses are "two" (Matthew 26:60) — a perverse inversion of the Mosaic requirement against the innocent.
3: "And I went to the prophetess, and she conceived and bore a son. Then YHWH said to me, 'Call his name Maher-shalal-hash-baz.'" The prophetic family as a living enacted parable foreshadows the holy family of the NT. The incarnation is itself the ultimate enacted parable: the eternal Word takes flesh in a human family (Luke 2:1-7). Isaiah's family of sign-children (Shear-jashub, Maher-shalal-hash-baz) is the OT precursor to the incarnate Immanuel who is the sign of all signs.
4: "For before the boy knows how to cry 'My father' or 'My mother,' the wealth of Damascus and the spoil of Samaria will be carried away before the king of Assyria." The sign's historical fulfillment (Assyrian conquest of Damascus, 732 BCE) establishes the prophetic word's reliability. Luke 1:1-4 opens with the same concern for historical reliability. The historical grounding of Isaiah's near signs grounds the credibility of the far signs about Immanuel — if this came true in verifiable history, so will the rest.
5: YHWH spoke to Isaiah again, adding to the oracle. The cumulative speaking — oracle upon oracle — is the prophetic patience that Hebrews 1:1 describes: "Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets." The "many times and many ways" is the narrative of Isaiah 7-8 in miniature. "But in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son" — the multiplicity of prophetic words converges into the single Word made flesh.
6: "Because this people has refused the waters of Shiloah that flow gently, and rejoice over Rezin and the son of Remaliah." John 9:7 identifies the pool of Siloam (Siloam, translating šilōaḥ, "sent") as the site of the healing of the man born blind — Jesus sends him there to wash. The people who rejected the gentle waters of Shiloah (Isaiah 8:6) are the ones who reject the one who offers living water (John 4:10). The "sent" etymology connects to John's repeated use of "sent" for Jesus: "the Father who sent me."
7: "Therefore the Lord is bringing up against them the waters of the River, mighty and many, the king of Assyria and all his glory." The rejection of Shiloah's gentle waters brings the Euphrates flood. In NT apocalyptic, the demonic flood of Revelation 12:15-16 parallels this: the dragon pours water like a river after the woman, to sweep her away — but the earth swallows the flood. Christ's mastery over the sea (Mark 4:39: "peace, be still"; Matthew 14:25-26: walking on water) demonstrates sovereignty over the flood-waters that represent both judgment and hostile powers.
8: "And it will sweep into Judah, it will overflow and pass on, reaching even to the neck, and its outspread wings will fill the breadth of your land, O Immanuel." The flood reaching to the neck and the address "O Immanuel" is the pivot: the flood threatens the land but the land is named for Immanuel. John 16:33 — "in the world you will have tribulation, but take heart; I have overcome the world" — reproduces this logic exactly. The flood that reaches the neck of Zion is the cross that nearly swallowed the Christ — but did not.
9: "Be broken, you peoples, and be shattered; give ear, all you far countries; strap on your armor and be shattered." The repeated command to the nations to arm themselves and be shattered is prophetic irony. Revelation 19:19-21 fulfills this: "the beast and the kings of the earth with their armies gathered to make war against him who was sitting on the horse and against his army. And the beast was captured." The nations gather against Immanuel and are shattered — the exact pattern of Psalms 2:2-4 ("the kings of the earth set themselves against YHWH and against his Anointed... he who sits in the heavens laughs").
10: "Take counsel together, but it will come to nothing; speak a word, but it will not stand, for God is with us (ʿimmānû ʾēl)." The Immanuel refrain is here explicitly rendered as a declaration: plans fail because "God is with us." Romans 8:31: "If God is for us, who can be against us?" Romans 8:38-39: "neither death nor life... nor any other created thing will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord." The ʿimmānû ʾēl is the Christological principle that no counsel against the people of Christ can prevail.
11: "For YHWH spoke thus to me with his strong hand upon me, and warned me not to walk in the way of this people." The divine grip on the prophet ("his strong hand upon me") is prophetic compulsion. Jesus's own "I must" language — "I must proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns also; for I was sent for this purpose" (Luke 4:43) — is the full embodiment of this compulsion. The Servant is driven by divine necessity, not by the desire for popular approval.
12: "Do not call conspiracy all that this people calls conspiracy, and do not fear what they fear, nor be in dread." The prophetic counter-culture: while the political class sees conspiracies and fears coalitions, the prophet is to fear YHWH alone. Jesus's "do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul" (Matthew 10:28) is the NT expression. Acts 18:9-10: "do not be afraid, but go on speaking... for I am with you" — the Immanuel-presence banishes the political fears of Isaiah 8:12.
13: "But YHWH of hosts, him you shall honor as holy. Let him be your fear, and let him be your dread." 1 Peter 3:15 cites this verse directly: "in your hearts honor Christ the Lord as holy" (ton Christon hagiasate) — the YHWH-fear of Isaiah 8:13 is explicitly Christ-fear in Peter's application. Revelation 1:17-18 — "when I saw him, I fell at his feet as though dead... 'Fear not, I am the first and the last, and the living one'" — the holy fear before the risen Christ resolves into reassurance.
14: "And he will become a sanctuary and a stone of offense and a rock of stumbling to both houses of Israel, a trap and a snare to the inhabitants of Jerusalem." Romans 9:33 and 1 Peter 2:8 both cite this verse as fulfilled in the rejection of Christ: "'Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offense.'" 1 Peter 2:6-8 weaves together Isaiah 28:16 (the cornerstone), Psalms 118:22 (the rejected stone becoming cornerstone), and Isaiah 8:14 (the stumbling stone) into a unified stone-theology: the same stone that is salvation's foundation is the stone that causes those who refuse it to fall.
15: "And many shall stumble on it. They shall fall and be broken; they shall be snared and taken." Luke 20:18 — "everyone who falls on that stone will be broken to pieces, and when it falls on anyone, it will crush him" — applies the stumbling-stone imagery to the parable of the tenants. The division between those built on the rock (Matthew 7:24-25) and those who stumble on it is the Christological fork in every human destiny: the same stone that is the foundation of salvation is the stone of judgment for those who reject it.
16: "Bind up the testimony; seal the teaching among my disciples." The sealed testimony entrusted to disciples (limmudîm, the taught ones — same word as Isaiah 50:4) is the prototype of apostolic tradition. 2 Timothy 1:14 — "guard the good deposit entrusted to you, by the Holy Spirit who dwells within us" — continues this sealing-and-guarding function. Revelation 5:1-5: the sealed scroll of divine counsel that only the Lion-Lamb can open. The disciples of the Servant are the bearers of the sealed testimony across time.
17: "I will wait for YHWH, who is hiding his face from the house of Jacob, and I will hope in him." Hebrews 2:13 explicitly quotes this verse as Christ speaking: "I will put my trust in him." The hiding of the divine face (hammastîr pānāyw) reaches its extreme expression in Jesus's cry from the cross: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matthew 27:46). The Servant who waits for YHWH while his face is hidden is the Christ who maintains trust through the God-forsakenness of the cross.
18: "Behold, I and the children whom YHWH has given me are signs and portents in Israel from YHWH of hosts." Hebrews 2:13 quotes this verse immediately after Isaiah 8:17, presenting Christ as the speaker: the Servant's sign-children are the type of Christ's "children" — those given to him by the Father (John 6:37: "all that the Father gives me will come to me"; Isaiah 17:6: "the people whom you gave me out of the world"). The "children" of Isaiah 8:18 are the Church, given to the Son, who are signs of the new creation.
19: "And when they say to you, 'Inquire of the mediums and the necromancers who chirp and mutter,' should not a people inquire of their God? Should they inquire of the dead on behalf of the living?" The contrast between consulting the dead and inquiring of the living God is resolved definitively in the resurrection. John 11:25 — "I am the resurrection and the life; whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live" — establishes Christ as the answer to the "inquiry of the dead." Hebrews 7:25: "he always lives to make intercession for them" — the living one holds the keys to death (Revelation 1:18).
20: "To the teaching and to the testimony! If they will not speak according to this word, it is because they have no dawn." The Torah and testimony as the standard — "if there is no light (šaḥar, dawn) in them." John 8:12 — "I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness" — presents Christ as the dawn-light that the teaching pointed toward. 2 Peter 1:19: "you do well to pay attention to it, as to a lamp shining in a dark place, until the day dawns and the morning star rises in your hearts."
21: "They will pass through the land, greatly distressed and hungry. And when they are hungry, they will be enraged and will speak contemptuously against their king and their God." The hunger and rage of the judgment era — where distress produces cursing rather than repentance — contrasts with the hunger that produces trust. The crowd that follows Jesus "because they ate their fill of the loaves" (John 6:26) but turns away when the teaching becomes demanding (Isaiah 6:66) reproduces the turning-away of Isaiah 8:21. Only the bread of life that Christ gives satisfies to eternal life (John 6:35).
22: "And they will look to the earth, but behold, distress and darkness, the gloom of anguish. And they will be thrust into thick darkness." The thick darkness that ends Isaiah 8 is immediately dispelled at the opening of Isaiah 9:1-2 ("the people who walked in darkness have seen a great light") — the death-darkness/resurrection-light movement. Matthew 4:15-16 quotes Isaiah 9:1-2 in connection with Jesus's ministry in Galilee. The darkness of 8:22 is not the final word — it is the darkness before the dawn that is Jesus, "the light that shines in the darkness, which the darkness has not overcome" (John 1:5).
